Let's talk about UTM tracking. So the name of the game is UTM and it's all about building links that are going to give you more data inside of your analytics and inside of your dashboard. So we've provided a link to come to the free URL builder provided by Google and the way that it works is this. You simply go here and you type in any URL. This is an actual link that you want to send people to. So wherever you want to send people put it right here. And as you can see, you're going to want to include the full HTTPS. And just to make sure that you don't screw this up, probably the best thing to do is just type it up here in the browser and then actually copy the link. So now that we've got that, what we're going to do is we're going to sign a campaign ID. So what this is is if you've got a particular ad ID that you want to attach to this link, you can include it here. Now if you're not running ads, you don't need to do this. And in fact, even if you are running ads, you don't need to do it. But just know that this is an ID like an actual number that you can include in here so that if somebody ends up purchasing or opting in, you can assign it back to that particular ad. But the fields that are most commonly used are these five right here, and especially these two, which are required to actually build a link. So your source is going to be your highest level. So this, as you can see, Google or newsletter or Facebook, here's the most important thing is that you create a standard protocol, like a list of names that you're going to give consistently because here's the deal. Sometimes if you put Facebook, or if you put Facebook, it can actually end up as different data. So it can be case sensitive. What I recommend is always using lower case and keeping this the most high level, like where is it coming from, the name of the website or wherever that might be. And then next is going to be medium. So how is the actual link given to somebody? So as you can see here, the examples, this could be cost per click ad or a banner or an email, whatever is the actual tangible unit that's used to deliver. So let's just say, for example, we're going to put Facebook, I'm leaving this blank. And usually, frankly, we do leave this blank. And then medium, we're going to put a banner ad on Facebook. And then the campaign name, let's say this is a lot of times what we'll do for this is we'll put the name of the offer. So, you know, for example, we'll put 10x book. And it's very, very important. Whenever you phrase this, every time that you have a similar campaign, that you use the same naming convention, because if, for example, if you were to one time name it 10x book and then another time name it 10x book, now you've created stats for two different campaigns and you don't want to do that. So I'm going to leave it as 10x book. So campaign term is primarily used for keywords. If you're running pay per click ads. The good news is, is if somebody finds you on Google search, it'll automatically put the term that they searched in this field for you. But in this situation, we're not going to fill this in because we're not going to be doing a pay per click ad. And then when it comes to content, this is actually going to give detail to what you're actually advertising.
